stem: A mountain road descends at a constant angle of 23 degrees below the horizontal from a pass to a valley floor. The road surface distance from the pass to the valley is 780 feet. A bridge crosses a river at the bottom of the descent, with the bridge deck 50 feet above the valley floor. Given sin 23° = 5/13 and cos 23° = 12/13, how far below the mountain pass is the bridge deck, in feet?
explanation: The total vertical drop from the mountain pass to the valley floor is found using sin 23°: vertical drop = 780 × sin 23° = 780 × (5/13) = 300 feet. Since the bridge deck is 50 feet above the valley floor, the bridge deck is 300 - 50 = 250 feet below the mountain pass.

distractorRationale.A: Computes the total vertical drop correctly as 780 × (5/13) = 300, but adds the bridge height instead of subtracting: 300 + 50 = 350
distractorRationale.B: Correct: Computes the total vertical drop as 780 × (5/13) = 300, then subtracts the bridge height above the valley floor: 300 - 50 = 250
distractorRationale.C: Uses tan 23° = 5/12 (derived from sin/cos) instead of sin 23° = 5/13 for the vertical drop: 780 × (5/12) = 325, then correctly subtracts 50: 325 - 50 = 275
distractorRationale.D: Correctly computes the total vertical drop as 780 × (5/13) = 300 but does not account for the bridge being 50 feet above the valley floor = 300

imageDescription: A side-view diagram showing a mountain road descending from left to right. The road starts at the mountain pass (upper left) and descends at a 23° angle below the horizontal to the valley floor (lower right). The road surface distance of 780 ft is labeled along the slope. At the bottom, a bridge is shown crossing a river, with the bridge deck labeled 50 ft above the valley floor. A vertical dashed line from the mountain pass level down to the bridge deck is marked with a question mark.
